items.correct: An object's design should be determined by its purpose.
items.misconception: Decoration is the most important aspect of design.
items.random_wrong: Designers must follow strict government forms.
items.why: Students invert the meaning. Sullivan's modernist principle prioritises FUNCTION over ornament - the chair's shape should serve sitting, not the other way around. Postmodernism later challenged this.

items.q: What is the significance of Duchamp's 'Fountain'?
items.correct: It challenged the definition of art - the artist's choice, not their skill, becomes the artwork.
items.random_wrong: It is a study of plumbing.
items.why: Students miss the conceptual point. Duchamp didn't make the urinal - he selected and presented it. The radical claim is that ART IS DESIGNATION, opening the door to all conceptual art.

items.q: What can line in a composition imply, even without representing an object?
items.correct: Movement, direction, emotion, and visual rhythm.
items.misconception: Only the outline of an object.
items.why: Students treat line as merely descriptive (an outline). In design, line is expressive - jagged lines suggest energy, curves softness, diagonals movement, even abstractly.

items.q: Which technique uses crossing lines for tone?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Cross-hatching. “Stippling” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
The answer examiners want is: Dots. “Lines” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How many primary colours are commonly used in pigment mixing?
items.why: Exact answer: 3 colours. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
